segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a man in a dark olive shirt, seated at a wooden table with a microphone, presenting data from printed charts. With a calm, analytical voice and a slight accent, he discusses gender-related trends, stating, "Something strange is going on with boys." He highlights academic performance gaps and suicide rates in various countries like Iran, Israel, the Philippines, Kazakhstan, and Finland, noting that "young men are more likely to be less educated than women, to die from overdose, drinking, or suicide, and even to vote differently." A subtle, melancholic piano melody plays in the background as he references a Financial Times report, explaining that "while young women are on average more progressive... boys are becoming more conservative," attributing this divide to "Gender equality." The scene then shifts to a split-screen podcast format, where the man in the olive shirt asks, "So why is this happening?" This is immediately followed by an aggressive male voice from a man in a black hoodie on the left, declaring, "Women are hardwired to get their needs met through a man." Another male voice, clear and assertive, from a man in a black jacket on the right, adds, "Women in America and London, how combative they are, and how hard it is for them to submit." The man in the black hoodie then states, "So, I think my sister is my her husband's property, yes." The video then shows the man in the black jacket shirtless with headphones and sunglasses, speaking animatedly, followed by two muscular men posing. The man in the olive shirt's voice returns, with the piano music, concluding, "Many agree that misogynistic influencers like these are radicalizing young men," as the final frames show a man in a plaid shirt with headphones and sunglasses speaking into a microphone in a studio with purple lighting.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Neil Shyminsky, a man in a dark plaid shirt and headphones, speaking directly to the camera in a simple indoor setting, stating that "Andrew Tate is definitely in the conversation for the most dangerous person alive." This transitions to Alice Evans, a woman with curly dark hair in a blue top and beaded necklace, who adds, "But I spoke to some experts who think there's a lot more going on." While the visual cuts to the man in the olive shirt seated at a wooden table with a professional microphone, Alice Evans's clear female voice continues, describing "Men who are struggling, who can't get status, who then feel insulted when this lower status group, women, is being assertive." Next, Richard Reeves, a man with short gray hair in a black blazer, appears in a warmly lit room, noting, "And while they don't always agree, what they have to say might surprise you." Neil Shyminsky then returns, emphasizing, "Men aren't wrong to feel like their issues aren't getting much attention. No, they're not getting much attention." A title card reading 'THE GENDER DIVIDE' appears on a black background, accompanied by dramatic, slightly melancholic string music. As the music fades, the man in the olive shirt, acting as a narrator, reappears and introduces the topic, stating, "I reached out to who I think might just be the best experts on this topic." He then introduces Richard Reeves, identified by a lower-third graphic as 'President of the American Institute for Boys and Men,' as "the president of the first and maybe only think tank in the world focused on the problems of boys and men." The narrator then introduces Alice Evans, identified as a 'Visiting scholar at Stanford University,' mentioning she is "talking with me from Seoul as she's traveling the world precisely to write a book on this topic titled The Great Gender Divergence." Finally, Neil Shyminsky, identified as an 'Author and Professor at Cambrian College,' is introduced as "a professor who interacts daily with the young generation and is famous on TikTok for his videos on these issues." The segment concludes with the man in the olive shirt, the narrator, inviting viewers to "let's jump right in."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a black screen displaying the white text 'WHAT'S GOING ON?' accompanied by a sharp, electronic sound effect, as a blue vertical bar slides in from the right. The scene then transitions to a dark studio where Chris Caurla, a DW Reporter identified by a blue lower-third graphic, speaks into a professional microphone while wearing headphones and gesturing with a pencil in his right hand. He articulately questions, "Lately, we have seen a lot of studies showing a growing gender divide. Some have even said that Gen Z is two generations, not one, because on some topics like gender, boys and girls have very different opinions. So, what's going on?" The scene cuts to a woman in a blue top and colorful beaded necklace, speaking directly to the camera against a plain white wall. She explains, "So if we look at South Korea, China, the US, and also across Europe, we see that women, young women, tend to express more progressive views, concern for gender bias and also racial bias..." The video then splits into a side-by-side format, showing Chris Caurla on the left listening intently, while the woman on the right continues with expressive facial movements, detailing, "...whereas young men, uh, not all, but some, may express concerns that gains for women's rights come at men's expense. This is the idea that women's rights might be a threat. And also in some parts of Europe, the idea of being concerned about migration or foreigners." Chris Caurla then interjects, "So, are young men just becoming more conservative?" The scene transitions again to a new speaker, a man in a black blazer, who appears in a warmly lit room with a lamp and wooden furniture, speaking directly to the camera with white earbuds, an engaged and animated expression, stating, "Well, it comes back to what we mean by these terms. Because there are other surveys which show that young men..."

segments.integrated_caption: The minute begins with Richard Reeves, a man with short gray hair in a black blazer over a dark shirt, speaking directly to the camera from a warmly lit room with a wooden cabinet and a lit floor lamp in the background. Wearing white earbuds, he animatedly discusses the ongoing need to address societal issues for both women and men, stating, "there's still a huge amount we need to do on behalf of women and girls, especially in senior positions like in politics and boardrooms and technology and engineering." He emphasizes that "it's not as if somehow all the problems of women have been solved... it is that we need to do both," and concludes by linking the challenges to "a world of floundering men" versus "a world of flourishing women," which he identifies as a central insight of "the women's movement." The scene then transitions to Alice Evans, a woman with curly dark hair, wearing a blue top and a colorful beaded necklace, speaking against a plain white wall. She asserts, "Absolutely, I think this is a really critical issue," and explains that "many people feel that they're struggling." Midway through, a brief split-screen appears, showing a man with curly dark hair and a mustache, wearing a dark olive button-up shirt and headphones, seated in a dark studio with a professional microphone and lighting equipment. The view quickly returns to Alice Evans, who continues speaking with expressive facial movements and gestures, maintaining eye contact with the camera, elaborating on the struggles by noting that "women face the largest gender pay gap in the OECD, they face massive discrimination at work," and also mentioning that "South Korean men have mandatory conscription."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Alice Evans, a woman with curly dark hair, wearing a blue top and a colorful beaded necklace, speaking earnestly to the camera against a plain white wall. She further illustrates this by mentioning her recent visit to Spain, where many young Spanish and Italian people are on temporary contracts, with some Italian men in their 30s still living with their parents. The scene then transitions to a split-screen, showing Alice on the right and a man with curly dark hair and a mustache, wearing a a dark olive button-up shirt, seated at a professional microphone in a dark studio. He listens intently, occasionally gesturing with his hands, before speaking in a clear, calm voice, questioning if the struggle is 'only in comparative terms' and clarifying that while young men are doing worse than their fathers, 'compared to women, they're still earning more.' The video then cuts to a full-screen view of this man, who continues to speak with expressive hand movements. The scene shifts again to a man with short gray hair, wearing a black blazer, speaking in a warmly lit room. He wears white earbuds and appears animated and engaged as he responds in a clear, slightly deeper voice, calling it 'a great question' and explaining that the situation 'does vary by country.' He highlights the US as 'unusual' due to an 'absolute drop in male earnings' for the bottom half of the labor force, contrasting it with other countries that have seen 'some wage gains.' The video returns to a split-screen, showing both men, as the man in the black blazer, smiling slightly, concludes that in all countries, there's been a 'significant shift in their relative position relative to women,' before his speech trails off. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Richard Reeves, identified by a blue lower-third graphic as 'Richard Reeves, American Institute for Boys and Men, President.' With short gray hair, a black blazer, and white earbuds, he speaks directly to the camera in a warmly lit room, animatedly discussing the positive consequences of greater equality, stating, "If women are doing better, we want that gap to close," and that it's easier for people to adjust to relative equality if they're "absolutely better off." The scene then cuts to a man with curly dark hair and a mustache, wearing a dark olive shirt, seated at a wooden table in a dimly lit studio with a professional microphone and headphones. He gestures expressively while reading from a notebook, explaining, "we covered education, we covered the economic situation, but there's more to life than the economy. So how are men doing on other metrics?" He references a study from "your book" (presumably Reeves'), noting it "found men who commit suicide were likely to use words like 'useless' and 'worthless' to describe themselves," adding, "which really breaks your heart when you read it." The video returns to Richard Reeves, who, with a serious and thoughtful demeanor, affirms the point, stating, "I think this is where a lot of the real tragedy lies," and highlights the severity of male suicide, mentioning, "in the US, we lost 40,000 men last year to suicide," and "four times as many men as women," reiterating that "the two words they most commonly use... are 'useless' and 'worthless'."

The Man in plaid shirt then responds, gesturing expressively with both hands, including clasping them together. The video cuts to a split-screen, showing the Man in olive shirt on the left—wearing a dark olive button-up shirt and headphones, seated at a table with a professional microphone in a dimly lit studio setting, listening intently—and the Man in plaid shirt on the right, continuing his animated and focused speech. The Man in plaid shirt passionately articulates his view on "toxic masculinity," defining it as "rigidity and intolerance to difference," and arguing that both masculinity and femininity become toxic when they "prescribe just one way, one role to operate in the world... and to demonize exceptions." His passionate and articulate voice perfectly complements his animated gestures throughout this remote conversation.

The video then cuts back to the man in the plaid shirt, who continues his animated discussion, adding, 'We just don't share a consensus...'

segments.integrated_caption: The video begins with the Man in plaid shirt, who speaks passionately in a simple indoor setting, gesturing expressively as he discusses the need to 'inoculate or vaccinate' against certain ideas and the importance of teaching feminism to children from an early age. The scene then transitions to Richard Reeves, identified by a blue lower-third graphic as 'Richard Reeves, American Institute for Boys and Men, President.' In a warmly lit room, wearing a black blazer and white earbuds, Richard speaks directly to the camera with an engaged and animated demeanor. He advocates for persuading fathers that they matter, even if they are not traditional providers, and stresses that paid leave policies 'must be equal for fathers and mothers' and 'independently available to fathers.' He continues, emphasizing the need for 'workplaces that are father-friendly' and a 'massive recruitment drive of men into teaching' and healthcare professions. The video then cuts to a split-screen, showing Richard Reeves on the right, continuing his discourse, while the Man in olive shirt, with curly dark hair and a mustache, listens intently with a serious expression from a dimly lit studio on the left. Richard Reeves further discusses the importance of 'technical training, apprenticeships, technical high schools' for boys, and suggests 'starting boys in school a year later' because they 'mature a little bit later.' The split-screen returns to a full-screen view of Richard Reeves, who concludes his points with subtle head movements and maintained eye contact, addressing parents, journalists, and think tankers.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Alice Evans, a woman with curly dark hair, wearing a vibrant blue top and a colorful beaded necklace, speaking expressively against a plain white wall. She critically discusses "regulating these algorithms that create such a distorted view," then shifts to a sarcastic tone, stating, "most of us are so normal, most of us are so moderate." The scene briefly cuts to a man with curly dark hair and a mustache, wearing a dark olive button-up shirt and headphones, seated at a table with a professional microphone in a dimly lit studio setting. He listens intently with a serious expression, occasionally glancing toward the camera, and interjects in a deep, calm voice, "And boring." Alice immediately agrees, "Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, most of us are really boring," followed by her laughter. The video then returns to Alice, who continues her expressive monologue, transitioning to an academic discussion about gender polarization being a function of cultural liberalization. She cites her travels to Uzbekistan, India, Morocco, and Turkey, explaining how "social policing" prevents men and women from thinking differently, and how "divorces are a humiliating failure." She then offers a nuanced perspective, suggesting that while polarization exists, it also reflects "growing freedoms and growing gender equality," before concluding that her "comparative research around the world shows that there are these very common structural drivers." Throughout, Alice uses hand gestures such as touching her neck and pointing, displaying a range of facial expressions from concern and intensity to amusement. Later, the screen splits into a side-by-side format, showing the man in the olive shirt on the left maintaining a focused, serious demeanor, while Alice on the right continues to speak with animated expressions and gestures.
